1:
"If there arise among you a prophet, or a dreamer of dreams, and giveth thee a sign or a wonder," The very brief verse notes the case of a prophet who gives a sign or wonder. The grammatical voices note the case. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "if there arise among you a prophet, or a dreamer of dreams, and giveth thee a sign or a wonder" — the case is put of a prophet or dreamer who offers a sign or wonder; the if there arise among you a prophet, or a dreamer of dreams (the false prophet or dreamer); the and giveth thee a sign or a wonder (the sign offered as proof); the figure (the case of the false prophet who works a sign to lead astray; the truth that a sign or wonder is not proof of truth if it leads to other gods; the figure of the test of the prophet, the wonder weighed against the word of God; the false prophet who may even give a sign, yet is to be tried by his doctrine, vv.2-3; the warning that miracles alone do not authenticate a teacher, "though we, or an angel from heaven, preach any other gospel," Galatians 1:8). The verse holds together the case of the prophet with a sign.

On "If there arise among you a prophet, or a dreamer of dreams," the witnesses note the prophet. Gill notes the case of a false prophet or dreamer (the test) — the prophet (or dreamer).

On "and giveth thee a sign or a wonder," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the sign: "and giveth thee a sign or a wonder" — the sign offered as proof (the wonder weighed by the word, Galatians 1:8); the figure (the test of the prophet by his doctrine); the providence (the case, the sign coming to pass yet enticing to other gods to follow, vv.2). The verse displays the case of a prophet who gives a sign.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the case — "if there arise among you a prophet, or a dreamer of dreams, and giveth thee a sign or a wonder," the case (Keil, Gill); "If there arise among you a prophet, or a dreamer of dreams," the prophet (Gill); "and giveth thee a sign or a wonder," the sign (Galatians 1:8, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the case. The verse marks the prophet: arising. The verse displays the dreamer: of dreams. The verse displays the sign: offered. The verse displays the test: set. The case displays the false prophet who works a sign. The verse displays the prophet: false. The verse displays the sign: given. The verse displays the test: by doctrine. The case of a prophet who gives a sign displays the test of the prophet: if there arise among you a prophet, or a dreamer of dreams, and giveth thee a sign or a wonder (Galatians 1:8) — the false prophet who may even give a sign or wonder, yet is to be tried by his doctrine, the wonder weighed against the word of God. The verse displays the sign coming to pass, yet the prophet enticing to other gods (vv.2-3).
2:
"And the sign or the wonder come to pass, whereof he spake unto thee, saying, Let us go after other gods, which thou hast not known, and let us serve them;" The very brief verse notes the sign coming to pass, yet the prophet enticing to other gods. The grammatical voices note the enticement. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the sign or the wonder come to pass... saying, Let us go after other gods, which thou hast not known, and let us serve them" — even though the sign came true, if the prophet enticed to other gods, he was not to be heard; the and the sign or the wonder come to pass, whereof he spake unto thee (the sign fulfilled); the saying, Let us go after other gods, which thou hast not known, and let us serve them (the enticement to idolatry); the figure (the sign fulfilled, yet the doctrine false, the enticement to other gods the test; the truth that even a true sign does not justify a call to idolatry, the doctrine condemning the prophet; the figure of the false teacher whose wonder is real but whose message is apostasy; the enticement to other gods the mark of the false prophet, however his sign succeed; the test that weighs the message, not the miracle, vv.3). The verse holds together the sign fulfilled yet the doctrine false.

On "And the sign or the wonder come to pass, whereof he spake unto thee," the witnesses note the sign fulfilled. Gill notes the sign coming to pass (the wonder real) — the sign (fulfilled).

On "saying, Let us go after other gods, which thou hast not known, and let us serve them," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the enticement: "Let us go after other gods... and let us serve them" — the enticement to idolatry, the false doctrine (the mark of the false prophet); the figure (the message that condemns the prophet); the providence (the enticement, the LORD proving them to follow, vv.3). The verse displays the sign coming to pass, yet the prophet enticing to other gods.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the enticement — "the sign or the wonder come to pass... saying, Let us go after other gods... and let us serve them," the enticement (Keil, Gill); "And the sign or the wonder come to pass, whereof he spake unto thee," the sign (Gill); "saying, Let us go after other gods, which thou hast not known, and let us serve them," the enticement (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the enticement. The verse marks the sign: fulfilled. The verse displays the doctrine: false. The verse displays the other gods: enticed to. The verse displays the test: by the message. The enticement displays the false doctrine that condemns the prophet. The verse displays the sign: real. The verse displays the message: apostasy. The verse displays the prophet: false. The sign coming to pass, yet the prophet enticing to other gods, displays the test by the message: the sign or the wonder come to pass... saying, Let us go after other gods... and let us serve them — even a true sign not justifying a call to idolatry, the enticement to other gods the mark of the false prophet, the test that weighs the message, not the miracle. The verse displays that the LORD was proving them by the false prophet (v.3).
3:
"Thou shalt not hearken unto the words of that prophet, or that dreamer of dreams: for the LORD your God proveth you, to know whether ye love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul." The witnesses note that the false prophet is not to be heard, for the LORD proves the people by him — the crux of the testing of faith and the supremacy of love to God over signs. On "thou shalt not hearken unto the words of that prophet... for the LORD your God proveth you, to know whether ye love the LORD your God," interpreters expound the test. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the proving — "thou shalt not hearken unto the words of that prophet, or that dreamer of dreams: for the LORD your God proveth you, to know whether ye love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul": the false prophet, even with his sign, is not to be heard, for God permits the test to prove their love; the witnesses note the significance — God's permission of the false prophet's sign is a test of the people's love and loyalty; the lesson — that the love of God is to be supreme, the test whether they love him with all the heart); "I will love thee, O LORD, my strength," Psalm 18:1; the figure — the testing of faith that proves and purifies it, "the trial of your faith... might be found unto praise," 1 Peter 1:7; "God did tempt Abraham," Genesis 22:1; the warning that the test reveals whether the love of God is whole. The figure of the proving of love to God, the test that weighs the heart against the sign.

On "thou shalt not hearken unto the words of that prophet, or that dreamer of dreams," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the refusal. Keil and Ellicott note the false prophet not to be heard, however his sign succeed (the message false, vv.2) — the refusal (not hearken).

On "for the LORD your God proveth you, to know whether ye love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the proving. Wesley and Calvin note God proving the people by the false prophet, to test whether they love him with all the heart, the trial of their faith (the test of love, 1 Peter 1:7; Genesis 22:1) — the figure (the testing of faith that proves it); the providence (the test, the call to walk after the LORD and cleave to him to follow, vv.4). The verse displays that the false prophet is not to be heard, for the LORD proves the people.

The witnesses agree the substance: the false prophet is not to be heard, for God proves the people by him, to know their love (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the testing of faith: the LORD proveth you — God's permission of the false prophet's sign a test of love and loyalty, the trial of faith that proves and purifies it (1 Peter 1:7; Genesis 22:1). The verse displays the testing-of-faith crux. That the false prophet is not to be heard, for the LORD proves the people, displays the crux: for the LORD your God proveth you, to know whether ye love the LORD your God with all your heart (Psalm 18:1; 1 Peter 1:7; Genesis 22:1) — God permitting the false prophet's sign as a test of love and loyalty, that the people's whole-hearted love of God be tried and shown, no wonder to draw the heart away. The verse displays the call to walk after the LORD, fear him, and cleave to him (vv.4).
4:
"Ye shall walk after the LORD your God, and fear him, and keep his commandments, and obey his voice, and ye shall serve him, and cleave unto him." The very brief verse notes the positive charge to walk after, fear, obey, and cleave to the LORD. The grammatical voices note the charge. Henry and Gill note that "ye shall walk after the LORD your God, and fear him, and keep his commandments, and obey his voice, and ye shall serve him, and cleave unto him" — against the enticement, the people are charged to follow God wholly; the ye shall walk after the LORD your God, and fear him (walking and fearing); the and keep his commandments, and obey his voice (keeping and obeying); the and ye shall serve him, and cleave unto him (serving and cleaving); the figure (the sixfold charge of whole-hearted loyalty to God, against every enticement; the truth that the answer to the false prophet is steadfast cleaving to God, walking, fearing, obeying, serving; the figure of the perseverance of faith, the heart fixed on God, "cleave unto the LORD with purpose of heart," Acts 11:23; the comprehensive loyalty that holds fast to God alone; the positive duty that guards against apostasy, the heart cleaving to God). The verse holds together the charge to cleave to the LORD.

On "Ye shall walk after the LORD your God, and fear him, and keep his commandments," the witnesses note the loyalty. Gill notes the charge to walk after and fear God (the loyalty, Acts 11:23) — the charge (walk and fear).

On "and obey his voice, and ye shall serve him, and cleave unto him," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the cleaving: "ye shall serve him, and cleave unto him" — the serving and cleaving to God (the perseverance, Acts 11:23); the figure (the heart fixed on God); the providence (the charge, the false prophet to be put to death to follow, vv.5). The verse displays the charge to walk after and cleave to the LORD.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "ye shall walk after the LORD your God, and fear him... and cleave unto him," the charge (Henry, Gill); "Ye shall walk after the LORD your God, and fear him, and keep his commandments," the loyalty (Acts 11:23, Gill); "and obey his voice, and ye shall serve him, and cleave unto him," the cleaving (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the false prophet put to death for enticing to apostasy (v.5).
5:
"And that prophet, or that dreamer of dreams, shall be put to death; because he hath spoken to turn you away from the LORD your God, which brought you out of the land of Egypt, and redeemed you out of the house of bondage, to thrust thee out of the way which the LORD thy God commanded thee to walk in. So shalt thou put the evil away from the midst of thee." The very brief verse notes the false prophet to be put to death for enticing to apostasy. The grammatical voices note the judgment. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "that prophet... shall be put to death; because he hath spoken to turn you away from the LORD... So shalt thou put the evil away from the midst of thee" — the false prophet was to die for seeking to turn the people from God; the and that prophet, or that dreamer of dreams, shall be put to death (the death penalty); the because he hath spoken to turn you away from the LORD your God, which brought you out of the land of Egypt (the crime, turning from the Redeemer); the to thrust thee out of the way... So shalt thou put the evil away from the midst of thee (the evil purged); the figure (the false prophet judged for seeking to turn the people from their Redeemer; the evil rooted out, that the people be kept pure). The verse holds together the judgment of the false prophet.

Gill notes the false prophet put to death for turning the people from God (the crime) — the judgment (put to death).

6:
"If thy brother, the son of thy mother, or thy son, or thy daughter, or the wife of thy bosom, or thy friend, which is as thine own soul, entice thee secretly, saying, Let us go and serve other gods, which thou hast not known, thou, nor thy fathers;" The witnesses note the case of the secret enticer, even the nearest and dearest — the crux of the supremacy of God over the closest natural ties. On "if thy brother... or thy son, or thy daughter, or the wife of thy bosom, or thy friend, which is as thine own soul, entice thee secretly," interpreters expound the case. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the nearest tie — "if thy brother, the son of thy mother, or thy son, or thy daughter, or the wife of thy bosom, or thy friend, which is as thine own soul, entice thee secretly, saying, Let us go and serve other gods": the enticer to idolatry, even if the nearest kin or dearest friend, is not to be spared; the witnesses note the significance — the love of God must be supreme over the closest natural affections; the lesson — that no tie of nature or affection may be allowed to draw the soul from God); the truth that the love of God comes before father, mother, wife, child, and friend, "he that loveth father or mother more than me is not worthy of me," Matthew 10:37; "if any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother, and wife," Luke 14:26; the figure — the supremacy of God over the dearest earthly ties, the warning that the most intimate enticement to apostasy is the most perilous and must be most resolutely refused. The figure of the supremacy of God over the nearest and dearest natural ties.

On "if thy brother, the son of thy mother, or thy son, or thy daughter, or the wife of thy bosom, or thy friend, which is as thine own soul," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the nearest kin. Keil and Ellicott note the enticer named as the nearest relations and dearest friend, the temptation heightened by their dearness (the closest ties, Matthew 10:37) — the case (the nearest kin).

On "entice thee secretly, saying, Let us go and serve other gods, which thou hast not known, thou, nor thy fathers," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the secret enticement. The verse displays the case of the secret enticer, even the nearest kin.

7:
"Namely, of the gods of the people which are round about you, nigh unto thee, or far off from thee, from the one end of the earth even unto the other end of the earth;" The very brief verse notes the gods of the nations, near or far, to which the enticer might draw. The grammatical voices note the gods. Ellicott and Gill note that "the gods of the people which are round about you, nigh unto thee, or far off from thee, from the one end of the earth even unto the other" — the enticement might be to any of the gods of the nations, near or far; the namely, of the gods of the people which are round about you (the gods of the surrounding nations); the nigh unto thee, or far off from thee, from the one end of the earth even unto the other (near or far); the figure (the gods of all the nations, near or far, the comprehensive idolatry forbidden; the truth that no idol of any nation, however near or distant, might be served; the figure of the universal prohibition of idolatry, every false god excluded; the enticement to any of the world's gods to be refused; the comprehensive sweep that leaves no idol allowed, vv.6). The verse holds together the gods near and far forbidden.

On "Namely, of the gods of the people which are round about you, nigh unto thee," the witnesses note the near gods. Gill notes the gods of the surrounding nations forbidden (the idolatry) — the gods (near).

On "or far off from thee, from the one end of the earth even unto the other end of the earth," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the far gods: "far off from thee, from the one end of the earth even unto the other" — the gods near or far all forbidden (the comprehensive prohibition); the figure (no idol of any nation allowed); the providence (the gods, the enticer not to be spared to follow, vv.8). The verse displays the gods of the nations, near or far, forbidden.

8:
"Thou shalt not consent unto him, nor hearken unto him; neither shall thine eye pity him, neither shalt thou spare, neither shalt thou conceal him:" The very brief verse notes the enticer not to be consented to, pitied, spared, or concealed. The grammatical voices note the refusal. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "thou shalt not consent unto him, nor hearken unto him; neither shall thine eye pity him, neither shalt thou spare, neither shalt thou conceal him" — the enticer was to be utterly refused, not consented to, pitied, spared, or hidden; the thou shalt not consent unto him, nor hearken unto him (no consent or hearing); the neither shall thine eye pity him, neither shalt thou spare (no pity or sparing); the neither shalt thou conceal him (no concealing); the figure (the resolute refusal of the enticer, no consent, no pity, no concealment; the truth that loyalty to God overrides every natural feeling, even pity for the dearest; the figure of the firmness against apostasy, the enticer not to be shielded; the duty to expose even the nearest who would lead to idolatry, no concealment; the love of God that suffers no rival, however dear, vv.6). The verse holds together the refusal of the enticer.

On "Thou shalt not consent unto him, nor hearken unto him," the witnesses note the refusal. Gill notes the enticer not to be consented to or heard (the firmness) — the refusal (no consent).

On "neither shall thine eye pity him, neither shalt thou spare, neither shalt thou conceal him," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the no pity: "neither shall thine eye pity him, neither shalt thou spare, neither shalt thou conceal him" — no pity, sparing, or concealing (the loyalty above natural feeling); the figure (the firmness against apostasy); the providence (the refusal, the enticer to be the first to die to follow, vv.9). The verse displays the enticer not to be pitied, spared, or concealed.

9:
"But thou shalt surely kill him; thine hand shall be first upon him to put him to death, and afterwards the hand of all the people." The very brief verse notes the enticer to be put to death, the witness's hand first. The grammatical voices note the judgment. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "thou shalt surely kill him; thine hand shall be first upon him to put him to death, and afterwards the hand of all the people" — the enticer was to die, the one enticed casting the first stone; the but thou shalt surely kill him (the death penalty); the thine hand shall be first upon him to put him to death (the witness's hand first); the and afterwards the hand of all the people (the whole people joining); the figure (the one enticed to be the first witness against the enticer, the hand first upon him; the truth that loyalty to God must overrule even the closest tie, the enticed himself the executioner; the figure of the zeal that prefers God to the dearest, the hand first upon the seducer; the public judgment of apostasy, the people joining; the gravity of enticement to idolatry, the death of the seducer, vv.6). The verse holds together the death of the enticer.

On "But thou shalt surely kill him; thine hand shall be first upon him to put him to death," the witnesses note the first hand. Gill notes the enticed casting the first stone (the witness's hand) — the judgment (hand first).

The verse displays the enticer put to death, the witness's hand first.

10:
"And thou shalt stone him with stones, that he die; because he hath sought to thrust thee away from the LORD thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt, from the house of bondage." The very brief verse notes the enticer stoned for seeking to thrust them from God. The grammatical voices note the reason. The verse holds together the stoning of the enticer.

11:
"And all Israel shall hear, and fear, and shall do no more any such wickedness as this is among you." The very brief verse notes that all Israel would hear and fear and do no more such wickedness. The grammatical voices note the deterrence.
